prior.title: EUDR (EU Deforestation Regulation)
prior.summary: DELAYED — now December 2026. Simplification review may change scope. ACTION NOW: Audit supply chain for EUDR-covered materials (especially wooden crating and packaging). Require suppliers to prepare geolocation data. Monitor simplification review outcomes. Owner: Compliance + Procurement.
prior.what_is_it: EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) under Regulation (EU) 2023/1115 requires importers to verify that commodities placed on the EU market have not contributed to deforestation. Covers: cattle, cocoa, coffee, oil palm, rubber, soya, wood (and derived products including timber crating, plywood, paper packaging). Due diligence statements must accompany shipments. Delayed twice — currently scheduled for application from 30 December 2026 for large operators. Simplification review ongoing.
prior.why_matters: EUDR directly affects freight forwarders handling timber crating, wooden pallets, rubber components, leather goods, and palm-oil-derived packaging materials. For freight forwarders: (1) due diligence statements must accompany shipments containing covered commodities, (2) wooden crating and pallets (plywood, softwood) are in scope as 'wood products', (3) forwarders must verify that suppliers provide geolocation data for raw material sourcing, (4) penalties up to 4% of EU-wide turnover for non-compliance.
key_data: Covered commodities: cattle, cocoa, coffee, oil palm, rubber, soya, wood
Freight-relevant: timber crating, plywood, wooden pallets, rubber, leather, paper packaging
Due diligence: geolocation data for raw material sourcing required
Penalty: up to 4% of EU-wide annual turnover
IT platform: EU Information System for due diligence statements
Delayed from: original Dec 2024 → Dec 2025 → Dec 2026
prior.reasoning: Affects any client shipping timber, rubber, leather, or palm-oil-derived packaging into/within EU. Freight forwarders verify documentation compliance.
prior.full_brief:
# EU Deforestation Regulation (Regulation (EU) 2023/1115) — Regulatory Fact Document

**Format:** Regulatory Fact Document | **Generated:** April 2026
**Primary legislative source:** Regulation (EU) 2023/1115 on deforestation-free products, EUR-Lex (CELEX:32023R1115) | **EC overview page:** European Commission DG ENV, Regulation on Deforestation-free Products

---

# Purpose and Scope of This Document

This document analyses Regulation (EU) 2023/1115 (the EUDR) as it applies to a freight forwarding operation whose cargo verticals include live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, high-value automotive, and humanitarian shipments, and whose transport mode priority is air primary, road secondary, ocean tertiary, across Americas, Europe, and Asia trade lanes.

The EUDR is not a transport regulation. Its compliance obligations fall primarily on operators and traders who place covered commodities and derived products on the EU market or export them from it. The workspace's exposure arises from two sources: (1) wooden packaging and load-bearing materials used in its own forwarding operations that fall within covered CN codes, and (2) client cargo that is itself a covered commodity or derived product, where the workspace may be characterised as a "trader" under the regulation's definition. Both exposure pathways are addressed below.

**Convention note:** The workspace is referenced throughout as "the workspace" or by operational profile. No individual, company name, or branded product is named.

**Dates confirmed in source:** Regulation entered into force 29 June 2023. Application date for large and medium operators: 30 December 2026. Application date for micro and small operators: 30 June 2027. Special carve-out: micro and small operators already covered by the repealed EU Timber Regulation (EUTR) apply from 30 December 2026. The regulation was amended in December 2024 and December 2025 to introduce simplification measures. The EUDR Information System launched 4 December 2024; user registration opened November 2024.

*Source: European Commission DG ENV, Regulation on Deforestation-free Products overview page. https://environment.ec.europa.eu/topics/forests/deforestation/regulation-deforestation-free-products_en. Accessed April 2026.*
*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, EUR-Lex CELEX:32023R1115, via EUR-Lex (id: 260089a9-e334-4104-843c-cdfc28a94dcc). https://eur-lex.europa.eu.*

---

# What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

## Plain-language summary

The EUDR requires any operator or trader who places covered commodities or derived products on the EU market, or exports them from the EU, to prove those products do not originate from land that was deforested after 31 December 2020, and that their production did not contribute to forest degradation or breach applicable local laws.

Covered commodities are: cattle, cocoa, coffee, oil palm, rubber, soya, and wood. Derived products include items made from those commodities — chocolate, leather, tyres, furniture, paper, and wood-based packaging among them.

The regulation repeals the EU Timber Regulation (EUTR).

*Source: European Commission DG ENV overview page, as above.*

## Why this applies to the workspace

The workspace, as a freight forwarder, has two potential exposure pathways:

**Pathway 1 — Wooden packaging and load materials used operationally.** Wood and wood-derived products fall within the EUDR's commodity scope. CN code 4415 (packing cases, boxes, crates, drums and similar packings of wood; pallets, box pallets and other load boards of wood) is explicitly within covered product codes under the regulation. A forwarding operation that commissions, procures, or uses wooden crating, wooden pallets, or plywood packaging on behalf of clients, and in doing so "places" that packaging on the EU market for the first time, may qualify as an operator under the EUDR definition. **Legal Confirmation Required:** whether the workspace's specific role in procuring or commissioning wooden packaging constitutes "placing on the market" under Art. 2(4) of the EUDR, or whether that obligation rests with the packaging supplier or the cargo owner, requires legal review against the workspace's actual contractual structure on each transaction type.

**Pathway 2 — Client cargo that is itself a covered product.** Across the workspace's cargo verticals, EUDR-covered goods may arise as follows:

- **Live events and film and TV:** Stage sets, props, and set-dressing elements fabricated from wood or wood-derived materials (furniture, flooring, structural timber) could be covered products being placed on the EU market.
- **Fine art and luxury goods:** Wood-framed artworks, luxury furniture, and decorative objects using wood, leather (cattle-derived), or rubber components may be in scope depending on CN code classification.
- **High-value automotive:** Leather interior components (cattle-derived) and rubber parts (tyres, seals) are covered derived products.
- **Humanitarian:** Wooden crating and pallets used in humanitarian shipments into or out of the EU are within the packaging pathway above.

**Legal Confirmation Required:** Whether a freight forwarder acting as agent (not taking title to goods) is a "trader" under Art. 2(15) of the EUDR — defined as any person in the supply chain other than the operator who makes covered products available on the market in the course of a commercial activity — requires legal review. The regulation's obligations on traders differ from those on operators in scope and due diligence depth. This determination is transaction-specific and must not be assumed.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, EUR-Lex CELEX:32023R1115 (id: 260089a9-e334-4104-843c-cdfc28a94dcc).*
*Source: European Commission DG ENV overview page, as above.*

---

# Issues Requiring Immediate Action

**WINDOW CLOSING — 30 December 2026 enforcement date for large and medium operators is now less than 9 months away.**

1. **Determine size classification immediately.** The workspace must establish whether it qualifies as a large, medium, micro, or small enterprise under EU SME definitions. The classification determines which enforcement date applies (30 December 2026 vs 30 June 2027). *Action: instruct legal or finance to run the calculation now.* The EUTR carve-out for micro/small operators already subject to EUTR also applies from 30 December 2026, which may affect the workspace if it was previously subject to EUTR in a wood packaging capacity.

2. **Audit all wooden packaging procurement.** The workspace must identify every transaction in which it procures, commissions, or arranges wooden crates, pallets, or plywood packaging for client shipments into or out of the EU. This audit establishes the scope of Pathway 1 exposure before legal review can be completed.

3. **Register on the EUDR Information System.** The EUDR Information System launched 4 December 2024 and user registration was opened November 2024. Operators subject to the regulation must submit due diligence statements through this system. The workspace should register now to understand system requirements before the enforcement date. *Source: European Commission DG ENV overview page, as above.*

4. **Legal Confirmation Required — role classification.** Commission legal counsel to confirm whether the workspace is an "operator," a "trader," or neither under the EUDR in each transaction type (packaging procurement, freight forwarding for covered cargo, export from EU on behalf of clients). Do not assume role classification.

5. **Insert EUDR compliance flow-down clauses in client contracts for covered cargo.** If the workspace is determined to be a trader under the EUDR, it will need to receive due diligence reference numbers from operators upstream. Contracts with clients shipping potentially covered goods should be reviewed and updated to require the client (as operator) to provide the necessary documentation. *Legal Confirmation Required: specific clause language.*

---

# How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

The EUDR creates a two-tier obligation structure:

**Operators** — the first entity to place a covered product on the EU market, or to export it. Operators must conduct full due diligence: collect geolocation data for the plots of land where commodities were produced, conduct a risk assessment, and take risk mitigation measures before submitting a due diligence statement via the EUDR Information System.

**Traders** — entities further down the supply chain who make covered products available on the market in a commercial context but are not the first placer. Traders have lighter obligations: they must be able to trace products to the operator's due diligence statement rather than independently conduct full due diligence (with a limited exception for micro/small traders).

The workspace's position in this chain varies by transaction type:

| Transaction type | Likely role | Compliance obligation | Confirmation needed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Procures wooden crating/pallets for client shipment, crating first placed on EU market | Potentially operator | Full due diligence + DDS submission | Legal Confirmation Required |
| Forwards covered goods on behalf of client who is the importer/placer | Potentially trader or no obligation | Traceability to client's DDS | Legal Confirmation Required |
| Exports covered goods from EU on behalf of client | Potentially operator for export pathway | Full due diligence + DDS submission | Legal Confirmation Required |
| Agent only, no title, client is the operator | Potentially no direct obligation | Flow-down clauses only | Legal Confirmation Required |

**Legal Confirmation Required:** Every role classification in the table above is provisional. The EUDR's definition of "placing on the market" and "making available on the market" under Art. 2 must be applied to the workspace's actual contractual and operational structure in each scenario. This is not a determination this document can make.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, EUR-Lex CELEX:32023R1115 (id: 260089a9-e334-4104-843c-cdfc28a94dcc).*

---

# Authoritative Guidance Document Analysis

*No content for this section as of April 2026: The European Commission has published an EUDR guidance document and an FAQ (listed on the DG ENV overview page), and guidance specific to the cocoa supply chain. However, the full text of these guidance documents was not available in the source material provided for this regeneration. Freight forwarder-specific guidance addressing the operator/trader distinction for forwarding agents has not been confirmed as published. The workspace should retrieve and analyse the Commission's published guidance directly from the EUDR Information System and DG ENV publications page before the 30 December 2026 enforcement date.*

---

# Anticipated Authoritative Guidance and Pending Regulatory Events

**MONITORING — Country benchmarking implementing regulation.**

The EC overview page confirms that an Implementing Regulation has been issued classifying countries according to their deforestation risk for each covered commodity. Country benchmarking directly affects the due diligence intensity required: products sourced from high-risk countries require more intensive due diligence than those from low-risk or standard-risk countries.

- **What it means for the workspace:** Shipments of covered goods or packaging materials sourced from high-risk-classified countries will require more documentation from clients or suppliers. The workspace's Asia and Americas trade lanes are directly relevant given the geographic distribution of key commodity-producing regions.
- **What to watch:** The benchmarking implementing regulation is published; the workspace should retrieve the current country risk classifications from the DG ENV implementation resources. **Research gap:** The specific country classifications and their effective date were not reproduced in the source material available for this regeneration and cannot be confirmed inline.

*Source: European Commission DG ENV overview page, as above — confirms existence of the benchmarking Implementing Regulation and the Strategic Framework Staff Working Document.*

**MONITORING — December 2025 simplification amendments.**

The DG ENV page confirms the regulation was amended in December 2024 and December 2025 to introduce simplification measures intended to reduce administrative costs and burden. The specific provisions of these amendments were not reproduced in the available source material.

- **Research gap:** The text of the December 2025 amendment has not been confirmed inline. The workspace should retrieve the amendment text from EUR-Lex to confirm whether any simplification measures affect the forwarding operator/trader distinction or the wooden packaging scope.

*Source: European Commission DG ENV overview page, as above.*

---

# Threshold Questions

The following questions determine whether and how the EUDR applies to the workspace's operations. Questions are marked as answerable from available sources or as requiring legal confirmation.

**1. Does wooden packaging used in forwarding operations constitute "placing on the market"?**
- The EUDR defines "placing on the market" as making a product available for the first time on the EU market in the course of a commercial activity, whether in return for payment or free of charge (Art. 2(4)).
- **Legal Confirmation Required.** Whether a freight forwarder that sources and applies wooden crating to a client's shipment is the entity "placing" that crating on the market — versus the crating manufacturer or the cargo owner — depends on the contractual structure and beneficial ownership at the moment of market entry. This is a legal determination.

**2. Is the workspace a "trader" for forwarding of covered client cargo?**
- **Legal Confirmation Required.** The trader definition (Art. 2(15)) catches any person in the supply chain other than the operator who makes covered products available on the market in the course of a commercial activity. Whether a freight forwarder acting as agent without taking title constitutes "making available" is not resolved in the source material available and requires legal analysis.

**3. Does the regulation apply to re-exports (covered goods transiting the EU)?**
- **Research gap.** The source material confirms the regulation covers goods placed on the EU market and goods exported from the EU. Whether transit shipments or temporary admission (e.g., fine art or live event equipment entering the EU temporarily) triggers obligations has not been confirmed in the available source material. **Legal Confirmation Required.**

**4. Does temporary importation of wooden crating (e.g., reusable cases for live events or fine art) trigger obligations?**
- **Research gap.** Reusable wooden flight cases and custom crates are standard packaging in the live events, fine art, and film and TV verticals. Whether temporarily imported wooden packaging — not "placed on the market" in the commercial sense — falls within scope has not been confirmed in the available source material. **Legal Confirmation Required.**

**5. Which size classification applies to the workspace?**
- Answerable internally. The workspace must apply EU SME thresholds (staff headcount, annual turnover, balance sheet) to determine whether it is large/medium (enforcement from 30 December 2026) or micro/small (enforcement from 30 June 2027, with the EUTR exception noted above).

---

# Substantive Requirements

The following requirements apply to operators under the EUDR. Whether and to what extent they apply to the workspace depends on legal role confirmation above. They are presented here so the workspace understands what it may be required to build toward.

## Due diligence system (operators)

Operators must establish and maintain a due diligence system comprising three elements:

1. **Information collection:** Product description (including CN code), country of production, geolocation coordinates of all plots of land where the relevant commodity was produced (latitude/longitude, polygon for plots over 4 hectares), quantity, and name and contact of the supplier and the buyer.

2. **Risk assessment:** Assessment of the risk that the product is non-compliant, based on information collected, country benchmarking classification, prevalence of deforestation in the source country/region, complexity of supply chain, presence of indigenous peoples, rule-of-law indicators, and other factors specified in the regulation.

3. **Risk mitigation:** Where risk is not negligible, operators must take measures to mitigate it (requiring additional information, independent audits, supporting suppliers) before placing the product on the market.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, EUR-Lex CELEX:32023R1115 (id: 260089a9-e334-4104-843c-cdfc28a94dcc).*

## Due diligence statement (operators)

Before placing a covered product on the EU market or exporting it, operators must submit a due diligence statement (DDS) via the EUDR Information System. The DDS reference number must be provided to the downstream trader.

*Source: European Commission DG ENV overview page — EUDR Information System launched 4 December 2024.*

## Traceability obligation (traders)

Traders must collect and retain the DDS reference numbers of the operators from whom they sourced covered products, and be able to make them available to competent authorities on request.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, EUR-Lex CELEX:32023R1115 (id: 260089a9-e334-4104-843c-cdfc28a94dcc).*

## Deforestation-free and legality standard

Products must be: (a) deforestation-free — produced on land not subject to deforestation after 31 December 2020; (b) produced in accordance with the relevant legislation of the country of production (including land use rights, environmental, labour, human rights, and tax laws); and (c) covered by a due diligence statement.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, EUR-Lex CELEX:32023R1115 (id: 260089a9-e334-4104-843c-cdfc28a94dcc).*

## Penalties

The regulation requires Member States to establish effective, proportionate, and dissuasive penalties for non-compliance. The regulation specifies that penalties must include fines of at least 4% of the annual EU turnover of the legal person. Specific national penalty regimes are set by Member States. **Research gap:** Individual Member State penalty regulations were not available in the source material.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, EUR-Lex CELEX:32023R1115 (id: 260089a9-e334-4104-843c-cdfc28a94dcc).*

---

# Registration and Reporting Obligations

## EUDR Information System registration

The EUDR Information System (Art. 33 of the regulation) launched 4 December 2024. User registration was opened in November 2024. Operators subject to the regulation submit due diligence statements through this system. The workspace should register and test system access ahead of the 30 December 2026 enforcement date regardless of role classification outcome, to avoid last-minute system access issues.

*Source: European Commission DG ENV overview page, as above.*

## Country competent authority notifications

EU Member States must designate competent authorities responsible for the regulation. The workspace will need to identify the relevant national competent authority for each EU Member State in which it operates as an operator (if applicable), as enforcement and audit requests will come from those authorities.

*Source: European Commission DG ENV overview page — Designated Member States' Competent Authorities list published.*

**Research gap:** The specific competent authority designations for each Member State relevant to the workspace's European operations were not available in the source material provided. The workspace should retrieve the DG ENV published list of designated competent authorities.

---

# Operational System Requirements

The following systems and processes need to be built, modified, or confirmed before 30 December 2026 (large/medium operators) or 30 June 2027 (micro/small operators, subject to the EUTR exception).

## 1. Wooden packaging supply chain tracking

**Scope:** All wooden crating, pallets, and plywood packaging procured or commissioned by the workspace on behalf of clients for shipments into or out of the EU.
**Gap from current baseline:** Unless the workspace already collects CN code, country-of-origin, supplier identity, and geolocation data for its wooden packaging suppliers, this gap is fundamental. Standard freight forwarding purchasing records do not capture geolocation of the production plot.
**Deadline:** Operational before 30 December 2026 (if large/medium).
**Action:** Identify all wooden packaging suppliers; request EUDR-compliant due diligence statements or documentation packages from each supplier now, given lead time required to obtain geolocation data from producers.

## 2. Client cargo documentation intake

**Scope:** All client cargo consignments that are, or may contain, covered commodities or derived products (leather, rubber, wood products, wood-framed goods) across all cargo verticals.
**Gap from current baseline:** Standard airway bill and house bill documentation does not capture EUDR due diligence statement reference numbers. A documentation intake workflow needs to be added to the booking and acceptance process.
**Deadline:** Operational before 30 December 2026.
**Action:** Design a commodity screening question into the booking intake process for all EU-bound or EU-origin shipments. For flagged cargo, require the client (as operator) to provide a valid DDS reference number before the workspace accepts the consignment for EU placement. *Legal Confirmation Required: whether this obligation falls on the workspace as trader or solely on the client as operator.*

## 3. EUDR Information System access and DDS submission capability

**Scope:** If the workspace is confirmed as an operator for any transaction type (most likely wooden packaging procurement).
**Gap:** If DDS submission is required, the workspace must have registered accounts and trained staff capable of completing and submitting DDS records before the enforcement date.
**Deadline:** Operational before 30 December 2026.
**Action:** Register on the EUDR Information System now. Assign a compliance owner for EUDR DDS submissions.

## 4. Supplier onboarding and contract flow-down

**Scope:** All wooden packaging suppliers and all clients shipping covered goods.
**Action:** Insert EUDR documentation requirements into supplier and client contracts. For wooden packaging suppliers: require provision of geolocation data, deforestation-free declarations, and DDS reference numbers or equivalent. For clients shipping covered goods: require provision of DDS reference numbers before consignment acceptance.
*Legal Confirmation Required: specific contract language.*

## 5. Training

**Scope:** Operations staff handling EU-bound and EU-origin shipments across all verticals.
**Content required:** How to identify potentially covered cargo by commodity and CN code; how to screen booking requests; how to request and retain DDS reference numbers; how to escalate anomalies.
**Deadline:** Before 30 December 2026.

---

# Exemptions and Edge Cases

## Temporary importation and carnet-covered shipments

Fine art, live event equipment, and film and TV production equipment entering the EU temporarily under ATA Carnet or customs temporary admission regimes are not "placed on the market" in the commercial sense. The EUDR's scope is tied to placing on the market and exporting from the market. Whether temporary importation of wooden crates or equipment with wooden/leather/rubber components is excluded from scope is not confirmed in the source material. **Research gap. Legal Confirmation Required.**

## Micro and small enterprise threshold

Micro and small operators who were not previously subject to the EU Timber Regulation benefit from the 30 June 2027 application date. If the workspace qualifies, this provides an additional six months. However, the EUTR exception means that micro/small forwarding operations already subject to the EUTR (most likely for wood product handling) lose this benefit and must comply by 30 December 2026.

*Source: European Commission DG ENV overview page, as above.*

## Products in transit

**Research gap.** No authoritative guidance confirmed in the available source material on whether goods transiting the EU without being placed on the EU market are within scope. This is operationally relevant for the workspace's air freight operations transiting European hubs on Asia-Americas lanes.

## Simplification measures (December 2024 and December 2025 amendments)

The regulation was amended twice to reduce administrative burden. The specific scope of these simplifications was not reproduced in the available source material and cannot be confirmed inline. The workspace should retrieve the amendment texts to assess whether any simplification reduces the due diligence burden for forwarding agents or wooden packaging procurers specifically.

*Source: European Commission DG ENV overview page, as above.*

---

# Confirmed Regulatory Timeline

| Date | Milestone | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 31 December 2020 | Deforestation cut-off date — land must not have been deforested after this date | **Reference date, in force** |
| 29 June 2023 | Regulation (EU) 2023/1115 entered into force | **In force** |
| December 2024 | First simplification amendment adopted | **In force** |
| 4 December 2024 | EUDR Information System launched | **In force** |
| November 2024 | EUDR Information System user registration opened | **In force** |
| 30 December 2026 | Application date: large and medium operators; and micro/small operators previously subject to EUTR | **ENFORCEMENT DEADLINE — less than 9 months** |
| 30 June 2027 | Application date: micro and small operators not previously subject to EUTR | **ENFORCEMENT DEADLINE** |
| December 2025 | Second simplification amendment adopted | **In force — text not confirmed in available source material** |

*Source: European Commission DG ENV overview page, as above. Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, EUR-Lex (id: 260089a9-e334-4104-843c-cdfc28a94dcc).*
